AS 6410A.description: This exercise is the integration event for Estimation and Kalman Filtering. Students will be given a scenario to evaluate the relative navigation system with multiple Kalman filter settings for a new satellite capable of rendezvous and proximity operations. Students will be given a test plan and measures to execute within the WebTools simulator and evaluate their system using a series of pre-planned test maneuvers. Students must apply knowledge gained during the Test Foundations series to plan, execute, analyze, and report on test results. After executing a series of test points, the test team will have to mission plan a new test point to utilize the remaining available velocity of the satellite within the simulator to get a more complete data set on one of the Kalman filters. Upon completion of their test points, students will complete and turn in a limited scope report based on the test data.

AS 6601A.eventName: Data-Driven Analysis and Control for Domain Sciences Lecture
AS 6601A.description: This block will cover advanced Data-Driven control techniques. It includes a broad scope of topics that build an understanding of aspects to consider when testing an autonomy-based system. Topics include identifying and analyzing different types of control systems and when to apply them, describing different categories of data and proper methods for their analysis, describing different forms and fidelity of simulation and their application, analyzing the use of Monte Carlo testing to include its advantages and pitfalls, and identifying key issues to consider when building up to and executing flight tests. It is taught consecutively with FQ 7300 Data-Driven Control.

AS 7110A.eventName: Satellite Attitude Dynamics, Estimation, and Control Lecture
AS 7110A.description: These blocks elaborate on nonlinear (and linearized) motion state dynamics (including autogenetic gravity center location); nonlinear state estimation; and nonlinear and linear state control (e.g. disturbance rejection). Lectures include time propagation of Euler's coupled, nonlinear moment equations relative to inertial reference frames and expressed in coordinates of the body reference frame. Luenberger observers are introduced as one option for nonlinear state estimation. Nonlinear feedforward control is introduced with dynamic inversion for feedback, optionally augmented by linear state feedback control. Structural dynamics are taught in the context of controlling highly flexible space robotics with hands-on elaboration on the robotic on-orbit trainer during AS 7140S GROOT Handling Qualities. These lectures can take place before or after AS 7150O.

AS 7111E.eventName: Satellite Attitude Dynamics, Estimation, and Control Assessments
AS 7111E.description: These blocks elaborate on nonlinear (and linearized) motion state dynamics (including autogenetic gravity center location); nonlinear state estimation; and nonlinear and linear state control (e.g. disturbance rejection). Lectures include time propagation of Euler's coupled, nonlinear moment equations relative to inertial reference frames and expressed in coordinates of the body reference frame. Luenberger observers are introduced as one option for nonlinear state estimation. Nonlinear feedforward control is introduced with dynamic inversion for feedback, optionally augmented by linear state feedback control. Structural dynamics are taught in the context of controlling highly flexible space robotics with hands-on elaboration on the robotic on-orbit trainer during AS 7140S GROOT Handling Qualities. These lectures can take place before or after AS 7150O. The lab provides additional details into attitude estimation and control design considerations, reference frames, Euler angles & quaternions, coordinate frame rotations, attitude sensor types / design, and an introduction to attitude control. Students will be expected to perform hand calculations related to the topics presented and create their own tools to perform repeated calculations. This lab provides hands-on experience using the Aerocube satellite system of the material learned in AS 7110A Satellite Attitude Estimation & Control.

AS 7120A.description: This lecture provides additional details into Power Subsystem architecture, details on solar panels, battery design, power distribution, conditioning & fusing, fault design, and power balance analysis. AS 7130L.description: Students will conduct multiple lab exercises using the EyasSat small satellite model aligned to the various satellite subsystems including the EPS, Structure, GNC and CDH. AS 7140S.description: Students will assess the handling qualities of the GROOT satellite in a variety of configurations and analyze the platform's stability in translational and rotational maneuver. Students will assess the handling qualities of the GROOT satellite including ability to perform the mission it was designed for. This exercise will require students to apply knowledge gained during the Test Foundations series to plan, execute, analyze, and report on their test results.

AS 7150O.description: Students will plan a series of passes to assess the Aerocube ADCS. Students will use their test results to develop an updated moment of inertia matrix for the satellite.

AS 7160A.description: Students will learn loads analysis for single degree of freedom systems; attitude trajectories; issues we create; deterministic artificial intelligence; exact linear parameterization vs. linearization; mass moments of inertia (matrix/tensor); feedback control and observers: and linear and nonlinear motion state observers. A 2-body mini-Groot example will be offered leading to understandings of structural filtering and culminating in a computation lab assignment: an n-body mini-Groot control with Bandpass + Notch and trajectory shaping. The culminating hands-on activity includes application to GROOT.

AS 9120A.description: This course is intended to provide students with a foundational understanding of the unique aerodynamic principles, thermal challenges, and vehicle dynamics associated with atmospheric re-entry from orbit.

AS 9130A.eventName: Re-entry Navigation and Low L/D Flight
AS 9130A.description: This course is intended to provide an understanding of the specific GNC challenges of guiding a low-maneuverability vehicle to a precise atmospheric interface point.

AS 9140A.description: This course is intended to equip students with a comprehensive understanding of the space environment, threats, and the systems used to maintain awareness.

AS 9201A.description: Lectures cover the five major threat threads of Co-orbital Anti-Satellites (ASAT), direct ascent ASAT, Directed Energy Weapons (DEW), Cyber Attack, and Ground Infrastructure Attack. This course will consist of a series of classified lectures and will discuss adversary threats with the potential to restrict US freedom of action in space and disrupt, deny, degrade, and destroy space assets providing critical capabilities to the warfighter. Synthesis event where students will perform a threat surface analysis of a space system against a specific threat thread and brief it to the block. Students will be assessed on their threat analysis and presentation.

AS 9202E.description: Lectures cover the five major threat threads of Co-orbital Anti-Satellites (ASAT), direct ascent ASAT, Directed Energy Weapons (DEW), Cyber Attack, and Ground Infrastructure Attack. This course will consist of a series of classified lectures and will discuss adversary threats with the potential to restrict US freedom of action in space and disrupt, deny, degrade, and destroy space assets providing critical capabilities to the warfighter. Synthesis event where students will perform a threat surface analysis of a space system against a specific threat thread and brief it to the block. Students will be assessed on their threat analysis and presentation.

FQ 6101A.description: The rigid body equations of motion for an aircraft are derived, and then trim solutions are presented. The equations are linearized in both state space as well as transfer function methods.

PF 6201A.description: Use of analysis, Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), and Wind Tunnel testing to predict aircraft performance. PF 7201A.description: These blocks cover the fundamentals of propulsion with an emphasis on flight test applications. It begins with first-principles analysis and applies them to various propulsion types, with a primary focus on turbine engines. Key topics include engine components, cycle analysis, performance evaluation, operability, and control characteristics. Students will assess propulsion system limitations, failure modes, and advanced concepts, including emerging technologies and alternative propulsion methods. The course prepares students to analyze, evaluate, and test propulsion systems in a flight test environment using analytical methods and practical applications.

PF 8101A.description: Equations that can be used to determine takeoff performance and to correct flight test data to standard conditions; equations to analyze ground roll and speed on landing, braking distances and the effects of reverse thrust, aerodynamic braking and runway surface conditions on landing; effects of atmospheric influences and variations in pilot technique at varying gross weights under various conditions; discussion of a variety of typical takeoff and landing tests accomplished at AFTC; braking, anti-skid and barrier testing. The brief will cover test techniques for performing takeoff and landing tests and gathering data for takeoff and landing ground roll distances; development of a method for reducing these data to standard day conditions; and safety considerations that apply. Though specific flights are not dedicated to collecting takeoff and landing data, it is expected that students will collect the data on all applicable sorties.

PF 8102M.description: Equations that can be used to determine takeoff performance and to correct flight test data to standard conditions; equations to analyze ground roll and speed on landing, braking distances and the effects of reverse thrust, aerodynamic braking and runway surface conditions on landing; effects of atmospheric influences and variations in pilot technique at varying gross weights under various conditions; discussion of a variety of typical takeoff and landing tests accomplished at AFTC; braking, anti-skid and barrier testing. PF 8110A.description: Theoretical background required to determine aircraft cruise performance; data reduction methods oriented toward providing cruise data in the format used at the AFTC; definition of the basic cruise assumption of level unaccelerated flight; development of lift and drag functional relationships based on subsonic aerodynamics in a form suitable for cruise performance analysis; determination of thrust and fuel flow parameters; derivation of the equations for range and endurance; determination of optimum fuel flows and RPM settings for cruise.

PF 8201A.description: Relationships of various forces acting on an aircraft in flight; the simplification of equations for steady climb and descent; factors that affect optimum angle of climb, maximum rate of climb and time to climb; analysis of polar diagrams for energy management; analysis of the forces acting on an aircraft in a turn; factors affecting turn performance; computation of corrections to standard day; methods of testing turn performance for non-standard day atmospheric and non-standard weight conditions; derivation and explanation of the equations used to correct check climb, level acceleration, and turn performance data.

PF 8203Z.description: Relationships of various forces acting on an aircraft in flight; the simplification of equations for steady climb and descent; factors that affect optimum angle of climb, maximum rate of climb and time to climb; analysis of polar diagrams for energy management; analysis of the forces acting on an aircraft in a turn; factors affecting turn performance; computation of corrections to standard day; methods of testing turn performance for non-standard day atmospheric and non-standard weight conditions; derivation and explanation of the equations used to correct check climb, level acceleration, and turn performance data.

SY 5101A.description: This 4-hour academic block is an overview of the essential math, physics, and engineering knowledge that span many mission systems.

SY 5102L.description: The lab introduces the student to test methodologies for evaluating integrated avionics systems in the F-16 UTD. The student is exposed to an F-16 Operational Flight Program (OFP) with a deficiency, enabled to learn about the OFP update process, and required to evaluate the OFP to perform limited regression testing to determine if new deficiencies were inadvertently created.

SY 5201A.eventName: Data-Driven Analysis and Decision Making for Mission Systems Lecture
SY 5201A.description: This block will cover data-driven testing for mission systems, including a broad scope of topics that include data science, uncertainty quantification, test & evaluation, statistical hypothesis testing, and decision making. Topics covered in the 1-week block include exploratory data analysis, data visualization, structured vs unstructured data, streaming vs. post-mission data, meta-data tagging, and structured inputs and data pipelines. It will also cover key performance indicators (KPIs), system performance and predictive modelling, validation and verification in data-driven test, statistical comparisons, Experimental design, adversarial machine learning & resilience strategies, and software for T&E of data-driven mission systems. Additionally, the block will cover probabilistic reasoning, reinforcement learning, autonomous control with RL (model-free and model-based), ethical considerations, data and decision fusion techniques, and considerations for tactical vs. operational vs. strategic environments.

SY 6101A.description: This 9-hour academic lecture course provides students with the theoretical background necessary to understand how ultraviolet, visible, and infrared electromagnetic energy is used in military applications. Several fundamental types of EO systems are presented, and their specific applications are discussed. Measures of performance and figures of merit for various systems are presented as well as specific ground and flight test methodologies used to evaluate performance.

SY 6110A.description: This 8-hour academic lecture course builds upon the Mission Systems Fundamentals lecture and provides the academic knowledge necessary to apply those fundamentals to development and test of military RF sensors. The student is introduced to radio frequency limitations, signal processing, RADAR technical measures, and mission-oriented designs specifically associated with military RADARs such as pulsed, pulse-Doppler, remote sensing and moving target detection.

SY 6120L.description: These blocks build on the RADAR academic knowledge with a hands-on laboratory and one hands-on simulator (6121S or 6122S). The first RADAR lab uses the TPS LabVolt laboratory RADAR to demonstrate theoretical RADAR/test concepts as well as basic RADAR displays (A-Scope, and PPI).

SY 6121S.description: The RADAR simulator occurs in the F-22 Software Integration Lab (SIL) for cleared students. Students learn basic and advanced RADAR test techniques for A/A and A/G RADAR.

SY 6122S.description: The RADAR simulator occurs in the F-16 UTD for uncleared students. Students learn basic and advanced RADAR test techniques for A/A and A/G RADAR.

prerequisites.name: RADAR Simulator for Cleared Students (US Only)

SY 6131F.description: This mission is designed to introduce the student to the differences between visible (TV), infrared (IR), and ground mapping RADAR sensors in the flight environment and to introduce several flight test techniques to evaluate the sensors both qualitatively and quantitatively. Knowledge gained during the EO ground test will be applied to operate a Targeting Pod in an inflight environment. The Sensors Flight provides data to support a written report due 5 business days after the last team sortie.

prerequisites.name: RADAR Simulator for Cleared Students (US Only)
RADAR Simulator for Cleared Students (US Only)

SY 6201A.description: This lecture covers RF and Communications systems fundamentals to prepare the students for the Communications and RF exercise. This lecture series will discuss the basics of data encoding, the link equation, antenna fundamentals, modulation techniques and multiple access techniques.

SY 6202L.description: The hands-on lab demonstrating RF and Communications systems fundamentals will be executed in combination with the Space Communications lecture. It will prepare the students for the Communications and RF exercise. The students will also gain familiarization with the software defined radio program, Hack RF equipment, and antenna connectivity. The students will gain familiarization with communications and antenna measures and measurement techniques. The demonstrations will step the students through a build-up of activities including RF Fundamentals using Software Defined Radios, Antennas and Common RF sources; Polarization; Signal interference and EW Measures; Antenna Patterns: Mainlobes / Sidelobes.

SY 6203I.description: The exercise is the integration event for Communications and RF instructions. The students will be presented a communications and RF scenario. They will work in small teams to develop the relevant sections of a detailed test plan which they will submit as a deliverable. They will then execute their approved test plan, collect relevant communications, and RF data, analyze their results, and submit a limited evaluation as a final graded assessment. The scope is estimated to be one afternoon of scenario presentation, RF lab equipment issue, Q&A, and deliverables overview. Two afternoons are scheduled for test plan writing and one afternoon for feedback on Test Plan and corrections. Two afternoons are slated for test execution, data collection and analysis. Two afternoons are scheduled for writing of the final assessment against the mission requirements.

SY 6301A.description: This 8-hour academic lecture Phase introduces aircraft navigation systems, including basic theory of operation, positioning information characteristics and basic test methodology. Topics include the basic principles of navigating across earth's surface, geodetic reference frames, ground-based radio navigation, Coriolis acceleration, inertial navigation, space-based satellite navigation with special emphasis on Global Positioning System applications, and integrated navigation and guidance systems using Kalman filtering techniques. The emphasis here is on understanding the fundamental principles of operation, what information is provided to the integrated navigation solution of the platform, and the limitations and potentials of these systems as they apply to systems testing and evaluation. There is also considerable discussion of the errors and analysis of large sets of navigation data.

SY 6303Y.eventName: Navigation and State Estimation Oral Report
SY 6303Y.description: Students will be assigned groups to analyze example navigation performance data provided by the instructor in the form of a notional navigation flight test program with specific requirements and performance specifications to be assessed. The students will present their results, analysis, conclusions, and recommendations to an audience composed of simulated stakeholders including the systems program office, contractor engineers, and operational testers.

SY 6304Y.eventName: Navigation and State Estimation Operational Topic Brief

SY 6401E.description: This test covers all the applicable component systems taught throughout SY 6000.

prerequisites.name: Navigation and State Estimation Oral Report
Navigation and State Estimation Oral Report

SY 6410A.description: These are a number of presentations on cutting-edge test techniques and technical details currently in execution throughout the Air Force Test Center

SY 7101A.description: This 2-hour academic lecture phase is a survey in the process of developing human machine interfaces. Benefits and challenges of each display type are examined and topics including resolution, touchscreen displays, helmet alignment, cockpit mapping, and test methodologies are presented.

SY 7110A.description: This is a 3-hour academic overview of the types of documentation and specifications common in software and networks mission systems testing. The MIL-STD-1760 interface and MIL-STD-1553 data bus protocol is introduced as well as other military and commercial data busses. This block introduces the various software development and test methodologies used by defense industry partners and the DoD for aircraft, space and cyber systems, and how requirements definition, development, and test approaches differ between legacy and more modern development frameworks. It focuses on how to create a software test plan that meets specification, performance, maintenance, and quality requirements while effectively managing the tradeoffs between risk, quality, and cost. Finally, the block introduces the DevSecOps cycle, Open Mission Systems fundamentals, and continuous integration and continuous development concepts as applied to software testing of aircraft and spacecraft systems. This block also discusses vehicles as a system of systems, then decomposes individual systems using the design process down to specific mission systems, mission essential functionality, and cyber-physical systems technologies. This provides a context necessary to understand how information flow within cyber-physical systems and physical technology shapes integrated avionics/regression testing as well as cyber vulnerability analysis.

SY 7111L.description: These labs reinforce academic knowledge acquired in the lecture with a 1-hour lab conducted with 4 students at a time. Students can manipulate code on a stand-alone computer to illustrate vulnerabilities such as buffer overflows and race conditions. They are also exposed to automated testing using CI/CD pipelines. Additionally, students demonstrate the performance differences between programming languages (compiled and interpreted), virtualization technologies (virtual machines and containers), Internet Protocol (IP) network data transfers (TCP and UDP), and cryptographic algorithms (AES and RSA) to better internalize the tradeoffs and promise of the cutting-edge computer science currently being used in modern mission systems architectures.

SY 7120A.description: This 2-hour lecture is an introduction to the unique testing considerations for unmanned platforms from a mission systems point of view. Subjects covered will include RPA common classifications, system components and traits, and unique test considerations.

SY 7201A.description: This 8-hour academic lecture course introduces students to the fundamental aspects of air-to-ground and air-to-air weapons and weapons integration testing. Topics include weapon components, guided weapons delivery testing, guidance modes, release point and impact point calculation, error budgets, safe separation, DT/OT teaming, some specialized weapons testing and instrumentation.

SY 7202H.description: The 2-hour field trip to the Edwards AFB weapons load hangar gives students hands-on experience with many of the weapons they may encounter during a test campaign.

SY 7210M.description: A 1-hour MIB outlines the flight and mission control room events.

During the sortie, students will sharpen their applied weapons test conduct foundations and will be introduced to weapons telemetry in the control room.

SY 7222F.description: This sortie is designed to demonstrate close integration with the Space Test Course Systems Practical and give select students a broadening experience for working with agencies beyond their background domain. Pilots selected should be primarily those with an F-16 background (primary) or those with a background dropping GBU-12s (secondary). FTC students will work closely with STC students to plan and execute a precision Time-On-Target GBU-12 weapon delivery to correspond with commercial satellite EO/IR pass. FTC students will also accomplish JDAM regression and DLZ analysis, along with any other regression cards in the time and fuel remaining. FTC students will assist STC students with any post-flight analysis. FTC and STC students will be scheduled for a 1-hour mission planning cell at minimum.

SY 7304F.description: The purpose of the F-15E/EX Advanced Sensors is to expose the student to a highly missionized manned aircraft cockpit. Students will be exposed to advanced EO/IR and RADAR systems along with weapons testing and mission systems control room operations.

SY 7312F.description: The purpose of the MQ-9 Evaluation Flight is for US students to demonstrate execution and debriefing of advanced sensor test techniques. Students will observe imagery obtained against non-traditional targets used for engineering measurements and collect qualitative usability data for a new sensor interface. SY 7521O.description: This practical will integrate the material presented in the mission systems platform course. Students will execute the exercise and complete a written report and oral presentation. Students will develop the test approach based on a scenario provided. Instructors will provide limited coaching and students will be expected to work in small teams to complete event. Multi-domain aspects of T&E will be considered. The project will integrate and reinforce the materials presented up to this point in the curricula and give the students an opportunity to execute a test project from beginning to end. Students will be tasked to evaluate a relevant space system. Students will be required to develop a test plan for review and approval. Students will then conduct the test using the assets available for the evaluation, analyze the data and draft a final report of the results. The scope of the exercise will be limited in order allow execution within the time constraints of the block.

SY 8101A.description: This 3-hour academic lecture introduces students to the fundamental concepts of communications and datalinks systems in modern aircraft. The course reviews the types of information that is communicated using communications technologies, introduces the basic technical performance measures shared by digital communications systems, fundamental test techniques used for all communications systems, and introduces datalink test fundamentals.

SY 8201A.description: This 2-hour academic lecture introduces data fusion models, methods, examples, and test techniques. The course builds on individual systems and sensors knowledge to teach how sensors can be integrated and fused into a user-friendly interface.

SY 8210L.description: A 2-hour laboratory is held in the F-35 simulator for US students. The Eglin F-35 simulator gives the students hands-on experience using modern integrated avionics.

SY 8301A.description: This 1-hour academic lecture will introduce students to the principles of advanced, network-enabled weapons test techniques.

SY 8401A.description: This 7-hour academic lecture introduces the student to basics of Electromagnetic Warfare (EW) and EW test and evaluation. The course then builds on the RADAR and communications courses with respect to those electromagnetic properties that support information flow to various sensors and how EW techniques can be used to interfere with that information flow. The course introduces the concept of Integrated Air Defense Systems (IADS) and the "kill chain" used by both offensive and defensive weapons. The course continues with Electromagnetic Warfare and EW testing, followed by Low Observable (L-O) technology and L-O testing.

SY 8410S.description: US students participate in a 2-hour simulator at the F-22 SIL to demonstrate concepts, capabilities, and test techniques in a multi-ship, elevated security setting.

SY 8411L.description: This simulator builds on the academics from the fundamentals and EW lecture to provide hands-on experience testing active electronic attack equipment in a laboratory environment.

SY 9120S.description: The students will participate in tours and receive hands-on experience in flight simulators with next generation fighter, bomber, and autonomous aircraft.

SY 9140A.description: The students will understand the physics of re-entry plasma and its effect on RF communications, along with mitigation methods.

SY 9150A.description: The students will be able to provide a scalable framework for understanding and testing complex space C2 architectures.

SY 9160A.description: The students will be introduced to the fundamentals of DE systems, their capabilities, and unique test challenges.

TF 5101A.description: The first block will teach students the fundamentals of Systems Engineering and how it applies to test. It covers complexity, systems theory, the scientific method, an abstract approach for decomposing the modern battlespace into test requirements, and a process for generating data and reducing system risk. The following blocks will then build on these modeling concepts and provide simple constructs for modeling systems from function to form. The written report provides an opportunity for the student to engage individually in Systems Engineering artifact development and communicate their purpose and importance to complexity management, test strategy development, and risk management using a notional system provided by the instructor. This project consists of a short technical report on a notional complex system, providing an opportunity for the student to demonstrate comprehension across a spectrum of Systems Engineering artifacts.

TF 5201A.description: The first block introduces Human System Integration (HSI) as a systems-based scientific methodology and highlights test considerations. It presents considerations for the human element cross- over into other all other disciplines. This block will introduce concepts of cognitive workload assessment and conducting HSI evaluations of systems. This block will also introduce qualitative and quantitative approaches to Human Systems Integration. The Model-Based Human Systems Integration (MB HSI) sim/lab orients students to human systems testing in a simulation environment. During a 1.5-hour test block, each student will receive a simulator orientation and individual simulator tasks. Basic Performance Resource measures will be collected and used in appropriate curriculum events for analysis. The last block reviews aspects of the human role in the operations process and describes the role of the TPS graduate in the Human Systems testing process. The block emphasizes space/aviation Human Systems history as it relates to major aviation, military, and space developments. The topics covered include control and display principles, along with cognitive workload assessment. Additionally, the last block covers basic functionality and limitations of human sensation and perception, as well as components of situation awareness, models of decision making, and case studies of both, mishaps and recent real-world test programs involving Human Machine Interface.

TF 5210L.description: The objective of this lab is to introduce the student to the science of Anthropometry (the measurement of human body dimensions). Anthropometric technicians will measure a variety of body dimensions for each student. Those dimensions will be placed into a Human Systems computer database which describes how students’ individual body dimensions compare to the historic US Air Force aircrew population. Each student will be provided a copy of their anthropometric measurements and percentiles, which can be used for future evaluations both at TPS and follow-on test assignments.

TF 5230M.description: The purpose of the MIB is to orient students to the scope and expectations of the Human Systems Evaluation Modified Technical Report and provide a dedicated opportunity for students to query the instructor regarding details of the report. Expectations for the system under test, data collection methods, and design of experiments guidelines will be provided. Students will work in groups to collect task performance data, evaluate the system interface, and provide design change recommendations in the technical report.

TF 5320A.description: (STC) Students will be introduced to DAF and OSD guidance on development of test strategies and TEMPs. This course will consist of a lecture, guided research using OSD or DAF-provided tools, case studies, and a student project. Given actual acquisition program documents, students will review a TEMP from the perspective of an oversight organization.

TF 5401A.description: This block introduces the framework which can be applied to complex systems and used for risk and safety analysis. This framework will be used throughout the curriculum for modeling and assessing a range of systems from simple to complex.

TF 5410A.description: These blocks provide the basic knowledge of test safety and hazard/risk identification, to include safety planning, Final Safety Review (FSR), test plan approval, test execution, and the project test plan change process. Test safety processes will be reinforced through practical exercises throughout the course. Students receive initial Project Safety Lead (PSL) qualifications upon completion of this course.

TF 5420A.description: This course presents the Functional Mission Analysis, a hazard analysis technique based on an extended model of mishap causation. Component failures, unsafe interactions, and social-technical systems − or the combination of all three − can be analyzed as a whole. Case studies of applications to past mishaps are presented. Students are led through applications to posed test projects to practice Functional Mission Analysis application.

TF 5501M.description: The objective of this mission is to familiarize the student with differences in photo, safety, and area chase. Students will practice or observe roles and responsibilities for a representative variety of chase maneuvers from both the test aircraft and the chase aircraft. These roles include test/chase communication and coordination, in the test role; precise flight test execution and management of chase, and in the chase role; flying techniques for a variety of chase challenges.

TF 6101A.parentCourse: TF 6000 Plan, Execute, Analyze, Report, Special Subjects (PEARS)
TF 6101A.description: The first block introduces students to the basic components involved with planning, executing, analyzing, and reporting during a test program. It provides context for the TF 6000 level of academics and introduces some of the basic “vocabulary” of aerospace test programs. The second block introduces students to the responsibilities of a tester, provides the context to help them understand where developmental testing fits into the greater acquisition enterprise, and introduces them to the processes which support test planning and execution. The third block will introduce students to the basic format and development strategy for Air Force Test Center (AFTC)-style test plans. It will explain the construct of specific and achievable objectives and detail the sub-sections that are hallmarks of an executable plan that will answer the customer’s questions. The oral report consists of a ten-minute group presentation that will take place a few days after the Test Planning Lecture. Students will analyze a provided test vignette, identify requirements and test objectives, develop a test methodology, identify a risk, and communicate their plan.

TF 6110A.parentCourse: TF 6000 Plan, Execute, Analyze, Report, Special Subjects (PEARS)
TF 6110A.description: This block provides students with an overview of test card requirements, best practices for test card layout, human factors design principles, techniques to optimize safety and data collection ability, and examples of test cards from various test programs.

TF 6120A.parentCourse: TF 6000 Plan, Execute, Analyze, Report, Special Subjects (PEARS)
TF 6120A.description: This block provides students with the basics of program management as applied to aerospace test and discusses the theory and application of theory of constraints (TOC) at the Air Force Test Center. Specifically, TOC is the method used to schedule projects within the constraints of available resources. Real-world test programs will be presented highlighting the project scope, resources, and associated risks (safety, technical, security, and programmatic).

TF 6130A.parentCourse: TF 6000 Plan, Execute, Analyze, Report, Special Subjects (PEARS)
TF 6130A.description: This block introduces students to the combat test framework which is intended to obtain combat realistic data for a system under test. Students will learn to leverage system decomposition, the integrated evaluation framework, and realistic threat-based combat scenarios to develop test events that produce combat relevant data.

TF 6140A.parentCourse: TF 6000 Plan, Execute, Analyze, Report, Special Subjects (PEARS)
TF 6140A.description: This block introduces students to formal Operational Test (OT) in the context of fielding new capabilities. An overview of the US laws governing OT and independent operational assessments will be presented. OT objectives and test considerations will be compared with those of Developmental Test (DT). Key OT terminology such as Effectiveness and Suitability will be presented, in addition to the different OT organizations and considerations for integrated testing will be discussed.

prerequisites.name: Test and Evaluation Master Plan (TEMP) Development (STC)

TF 6201A.parentCourse: TF 6000 Plan, Execute, Analyze, Report, Special Subjects (PEARS)
TF 6201A.description: This course introduces students to the fundamentals of test control and conduct and familiarizes them with content of AFTCI 99-105, Test Control and Conduct. It emphasizes the elements required for safe and efficient test control and conduct to include required personnel, mission preparation, test card generation, communications plans, execution techniques, and post-test debrief.

TF 6210A.parentCourse: TF 6000 Plan, Execute, Analyze, Report, Special Subjects (PEARS)
TF 6210A.description: This lesson introduces students to Time Safety Margin (TSM) calculations, demonstrates how TSM can be utilized for safe test execution, and equips the students to be able to plan their own TSMs.

TF 6220M.eventName: Control Room Test Conduct Primer MIB
TF 6220M.parentCourse: TF 6000 Plan, Execute, Analyze, Report, Special Subjects (PEARS)
TF 6220M.description: The MIB presents a flight test scenario composed of various flying qualities and mission system test points. System requirements, test objectives, and measures of performance from the provided test plan are presented to introduce students to an example of decomposing system requirements into test objectives and data requirements used to develop the appropriate FTT or test methodology. The mission profile, communication plan, limits, and control room screens are also reviewed. Utilizing the controlled environment afforded with a simulator, a mock flight test is conducted from the control room. Students are familiarized with the radio procedures and control room environment and are given the opportunity to practice control of a test mission to include basic test point pacing, data monitoring, and maneuver feedback. CSO, P, and RPA students have the option to sit in and observe the event.

TF 6232F.eventName: F-16/Control Room Test Conduct Intro Flight (optional flight for CSO/P/RPA)
TF 6232F.parentCourse: TF 6000 Plan, Execute, Analyze, Report, Special Subjects (PEARS)
TF 6232F.description: Utilizing flight test techniques derived from propulsion testing, students are introduced to the methods for conducting a test from a remote telemetry control room. This event builds on the control room resources introduced in CF 6621C Control Room Familiarization and concepts for control room operations introduced in TF 6221C Control Room Test Conduct Primer. This event also increases the complexity of test conduct with active test control and the real-world “fog and friction” associated with actual flight test.

TF 6240M.eventName: C-12 Airborne Test Conduct Intro MIB
TF 6240M.parentCourse: TF 6000 Plan, Execute, Analyze, Report, Special Subjects (PEARS)
TF 6240M.description: The MIB gives students information on test conduct techniques, test team coordination, and in-flight data collection. It also highlights mission events for a C-12 Airborne Test Conduct Intro flight that introduces FTC students to directing a test mission as a participating entity not in control of the vehicle flightpath. During the flight, an instructor will demonstrate test conduct techniques for a ride-along test collecting data from systems independent of the C-12 during a concurrent flight test of C-12 performance FTTs. Instruction will be provided on data collection techniques, test team communication, and the importance of understanding test execution pacing and timing. STC students will ride-along an airborne event which will be accomplished during existing FTC C-12 events when a C-12 IP is required, not including practical events (see list below). The MIB gives STC students information on airborne test conduct techniques, test team coordination, and in-flight data collection. For the event, students will conduct ride-along evaluation of a Time Space Position Instrumentation (TSPI) system. Students are provided with MPG/Test Plan/Test Matrix and are required to create test cards, evaluation methods, and track test matrix completion as a team. Instruction will be provided on data collection and analysis techniques for TSPI systems and a description of the flight test techniques that will occur during the ride-along events. Sorties that can be used for ride-along include:  TF 6241F C-12 Airborne Test Conduct Intro, PF 8211F C-12 Level Accel/Turn Perf Flight, PF 8302F C-12 Autopilot Flight Control System Flight, TF 6251F C-12 Intermediate Airborne Test Conduct, FQ 6241F C-12 S

TF 6250M.eventName: C-12 Intermediate Airborne Test Conduct MIB
TF 6250M.parentCourse: TF 6000 Plan, Execute, Analyze, Report, Special Subjects (PEARS)
TF 6250M.description: A C-12 based event in which students will work in pairs to plan, execute, and debrief a successful test. Students will be responsible for all aspects of the test, from mission profile development and flight card generation to timeline adherence and real-time decision-making during execution based on provided mission priorities. During debrief, students will rate maneuver data quality, discuss lessons learned, and present flight test results using provided performance analysis tools.

TF 6260A.parentCourse: TF 6000 Plan, Execute, Analyze, Report, Special Subjects (PEARS)
TF 6260A.description: The aerodynamics lesson describes the four basic forces of flight, Bernoulli’s Theory and Newton’s Laws, how a wing makes lift, critical angle of attack and stalls, how center of gravity affects aircraft stability, and why an aircraft turns. The pitot statics lesson describes the basic concepts of a pitot-static system and associated instruments, common sources of error in pitot-static measurements, error sources to data accuracy, and the relationship of data accuracy to test and evaluation results. The Low L/D guest lecture will provide students with background and lessons learned in developing and executing the low L/D profile in the T-38 aircraft and an opportunity to interact with an experienced T&E professional. The glider exercise consists of multiple lessons to prepare the class to execute a limited scope test, analyze the results and report on those results. The purpose of the exercise is to reinforce the preceding classroom lessons and introduce students to the dynamics of testing in a controlled environment with coaching from instructors. Student teams will need to operate in an unfamiliar environment and under significant time constraints. Students will prepare a limited scope test plan and submit it for approval prior to execution of the events. Students have the option to fly with an instructor on at least one data collection. Data from each will be compiled into a shared set of test data for the class. The exercise will include a risk assessment workshop (1.5 hr.) and Test Plan review (1.5 hr.). The last block will expose the student to test conduct in a unique environment while exploring additional concepts.

TF 6270B.eventName: T-38 Low L/D Flight/Control Room MIB
TF 6270B.parentCourse: TF 6000 Plan, Execute, Analyze, Report, Special Subjects (PEARS)
TF 6270B.description: The T-38 Low L/D Flight/Control Room event will entail STC students executing the T-38 Low L/D profile in the aircraft and/or the control room. Provided mission materials (MPG and test cards) will detail the event and tie aircraft modeling to glide performance. Control room screens will demonstrate how aircraft modeling and predictions can be applied to real-time monitoring. Pre-recorded T-38 control room data will be available for mission playback for scheduling scenarios where a telemetry-capable T-38 is not available.

TF 6280A.parentCourse: TF 6000 Plan, Execute, Analyze, Report, Special Subjects (PEARS)
TF 6280A.description: This block discusses test security considerations and presents some useful techniques for dealing with sensitive tests.

TF 6301A.parentCourse: TF 6000 Plan, Execute, Analyze, Report, Special Subjects (PEARS)
TF 6301A.description: This block will teach students how to use statistical techniques to extract information from test data. Specifically, the role that statistics can play in the test process is discussed. The following basic concepts are explained: variability, random sampling, designed experiments, mechanistic versus empirical models, enumerative versus analytic studies, and finally sample mean and variance versus population mean and variance. This block also teaches students to compute various statistics, display results, and interpret them. It covers the basics of probability distributions and how to use them. Finally, students will be able to apply the following basic statistical techniques to test problems: hypothesis tests, linear regression, confidence intervals, and prediction intervals.

TF 6310A.parentCourse: TF 6000 Plan, Execute, Analyze, Report, Special Subjects (PEARS)
TF 6310A.description: Building on the statistics learned and reviewed in TF 6301A, this block will teach students how to apply statistical and constrained optimization methods to designing statistically defensible tests while meeting programmatic constraints such as test hours, instrumentation costs, and total budget. The general concept of Design of Experiments (DOE) is derived using optimization theory and applied to the concept of designing a constrained test program that can answer the customer’s questions in a statistically defensible manner.

TF 6320A.parentCourse: TF 6000 Plan, Execute, Analyze, Report, Special Subjects (PEARS)
TF 6320A.description: This block covers techniques for presenting data in a variety of media to communicate a clear message to the intended audience. Methods of presenting data in a visible, clear, and convincing manner are covered along with common presentation pitfalls.

TF 6401A.parentCourse: TF 6000 Plan, Execute, Analyze, Report, Special Subjects (PEARS)
TF 6401A.description: This block introduces students to the concept of test reporting; specifically, why they write reports and where reports fit into the overall test process. It introduces the various types of reports in the test world and lists the reports that students will produce during the curriculum. Students will write an individual report using sample aircraft data and receive feedback. Students will also work in a group to collect data, analyze data, write a report, and receive report feedback.

TF 6410B.parentCourse: TF 6000 Plan, Execute, Analyze, Report, Special Subjects (PEARS)
TF 6410B.description: This course teaches students how to communicate test results in an efficient manner that meets the user’s need. It emphasizes how to organize and support technical discussions. It explains how to write technical reports and provides examples of different styles/formats.

TF 6420A.parentCourse: TF 6000 Plan, Execute, Analyze, Report, Special Subjects (PEARS)
TF 6420A.description: This block introduces techniques for reporting on test data in an oral presentation. Common PowerPoint tips and pitfalls are discussed.

TF 6430B.parentCourse: TF 6000 Plan, Execute, Analyze, Report, Special Subjects (PEARS)
TF 6430B.description: This block introduces students to deficiency reports, specifically what they are, who can submit them, how they are written, and how they are handled by the test organization, the program office, AFMC, and the contractor once they are written.

TF 6501A.parentCourse: TF 6000 Plan, Execute, Analyze, Report, Special Subjects (PEARS)
TF 6501A.description: This block introduces students to space acquisition/test and identifies the similarities and differences between space and air vehicle test. The student is introduced to the space environment, systems, missions, organizations, acquisition, and test. The block includes a discussion of space policy guidance and specific discussions of space developmental and operational test, including examples and real-world vignettes. This block also introduces classified space systems with a discussion of real-world acquisition and test considerations for such systems.

TF 6502A.parentCourse: TF 6000 Plan, Execute, Analyze, Report, Special Subjects (PEARS)
TF 6502A.description: This block introduces students to space acquisition/test and identifies the similarities and differences between space and air vehicle test. The block includes a discussion of space policy guidance and specific discussions of space developmental and operational test, including examples and real-world vignettes. This block also introduces classified space systems with a discussion of real-world acquisition and test considerations for such systems.

TF 6510A.parentCourse: TF 6000 Plan, Execute, Analyze, Report, Special Subjects (PEARS)
TF 6510A.description: This is a two day-long guided exercise provided by the Air Force Joint Test Program Office. The students will exercise the process that rapidly provides non-materiel solutions to identified operational deficiencies. The course will review operational test definitions such as Critical Operational Issues (COI), System Under Test (SUT), and Tactics, Techniques and Procedures (TTP), and Measures of Effectiveness/Suitability (MOE/MOS). The students will be posed with operational deficiencies and will generate a COI, and associated MOEs/MOSs. The students will develop an execution/data plan for a “test event” of a non-materiel solution (TTP). They will analyze the results and report on the findings.

TF 6520A.parentCourse: TF 6000 Plan, Execute, Analyze, Report, Special Subjects (PEARS)
TF 6520A.description: This block teaches students why all-weather testing is done, what the major objectives are, where these tests are conducted, and how to plan and execute an all-weather test.

TF 7101A.description: Students are introduced to the purpose of qualitative evaluations. Techniques are presented to help the students make the most of every qual eval, and specific safety considerations are introduced. The lesson emphasizes techniques on how to properly report on their flight and gives guidelines for the graded qual eval flights. Since many of the qual eval instructor pilots will be contractors, students are reminded of the sensitivities faced when dealing with contractors and are instructed on the proper way to interface with them.

TF 7102A.description: Students are introduced to the purpose of qualitative evaluations. TF 7110M.description: For each of the three graded qual eval flights, an MIB will introduce the aircraft and the mission for which it will be evaluated. The instructor pilot will provide ground school academics covering the major systems of the subject aircraft as well as any required briefing items and aircraft handling techniques. Following each qual eval flight, students will write a qual eval report documenting their flight and evaluating the aircraft for use in a particular mission.

TF 7201M.description: TF 7210-15G Experience Broadening Qual Eval Ground School 1-6 TF 7220-25F Experience Broadening Qual Eval Flight 1-6 Additional unique aircraft will be brought in and flown to expand the students’ experience base. However, no report is required following the flight. The single digit indicates required prerequisites for the ground school and flights (e.g., 7210G is required before 7220F, 7211G is required before 7221F).

TF 7230G.eventName: C-17 Experience Broadening Qual Eval Ground School
TF 7230G.description: The ground school will cover the required areas of operation for students to evaluate the C-17 in groups. There is no reporting requirement for this flight.

TF 7231F.eventName: C-17 Experience Broadening Qual Eval Flight

TF 7240S.description: Utilizing high-fidelity simulators, students will be exposed to two modern heavy aircraft of different designs to compare different design philosophies. In addition to experiencing test operations in a large aircraft, students will perform high-risk large aircraft ground/flight test techniques that cannot be performed inflight at TPS due to safety and aircraft availability considerations.

TF 7250G.eventName: Navy TPS Fixed Wing Qual Eval Ground School
TF 7250G.description: During a visit to the US Navy Test Pilot School (USNTPS), experience broadening non-graded qual evals may include an F/A-18 out-of-control flight demonstration (when available), a single-engine tail dragger for pilots, and/or a T-6B for non-pilots.

TF 7251F.description: During a visit to the US Navy Test Pilot School (USNTPS), experience broadening non-graded qual evals may include an F/A-18 out-of-control flight demonstration (when available), a single-engine tail dragger for pilots, and/or a T-6B for non-pilots.

TF 7301M.eventName: Intro to Rotary Wing Flight MIB
TF 7301M.description: This MIB will outline the learning objectives of flying rotary wing aircraft and basic warnings and limitations students can expect. The ground school will cover basic rotary wing flight dynamics and the different controls involved in flying helicopters to prepare students for their introductory flight.

TF 7302G.description: This MIB will outline the learning objectives of flying rotary wing aircraft and basic warnings and limitations students can expect. TF 7303F.description: This MIB will outline the learning objectives of flying rotary wing aircraft and basic warnings and limitations students can expect. TF 7401C.description: Students will observe a real-world flight test mission control room to provide them with a better perspective of real-world control room operations. The event will also help students better visualize the roles and responsibilities that they may have post-graduation.

TF 7501M.description: The student team will develop a test plan to evaluate an unfamiliar aircraft in a scenario that the students develop. Students will deconstruct the scenario into relevant qual eval test objectives and appropriate test techniques for obtaining the qualitative and quantitative data necessary to inform the test objectives. In addition to the test plan, test cards will also be submitted for staff monitor review. Upon returning from their capstone qual eval, students will present their findings in a brief oral report to the school. Students in the junior class observe the oral report. This event provides students with the opportunity to demonstrate their ability to plan, execute, and deliver an oral report on a qualitative evaluation of an unfamiliar aircraft. The student will fly a single qual eval sortie at the capstone host location to collect data for the report.

TF 7601A.description: This lesson is a precursor to the TF 5230M Human Systems Evaluation MIB. It provides familiarization with the WGS Technical Orders and other engineering documentation students will be interacting with during the exercise.

TF 8101A.description: During the TMP Assignments and Introduction Lecture, students will be presented with their assigned TMP groups. A basic overview of the 412 Test Wing Program Management process, expectations and timelines for the TMP process, and differences between the USAF TPS TMP process and 412 TW process will be presented. During the Resources Workshop, each student team will meet with the USAF TPS Program Managers, TMP Director, and their Staff Advisors to discuss their specific programmatic requirements and resources. Students will be instructed to draft a 412 TW Test Concept Document (TCD), identifying their TMP's overall objectives, any aircraft or pod modifications required, and support required from other organizations such as logistics, ranges, instrumentation, environmental, frequency management, and/or maintenance. The TCD will be required for TF 8103W. Prior to the Core Team Meeting, the student teams will send their TCDs to the USAF TPS A5 TMP Program Manager and TMP Director, who will review the TCDs for any final edits. The A5 Program Manager will formally begin the 412 TW PID/SOC process by distributing the TCDs to the 412 TW POCs and non-AFTC organizations identified by each student team and requesting a JON. During the Core Team Meeting, the student teams will present their TCDs to the respective organizations, allowing the organizations an opportunity to either concur or non-concur with the support being requested. During the TMP planning process, students will have two test plan working groups (TPWGs) to assist them with drafting their test plan, safety plan, execution schedule, and any additional documentation as required by their TMP. The first TPWG will focus on defining the general test objectives (GTOs), specific test objectives (STOs), measures of performance (MOPs), and any outstanding technical planning discussions that will feed directly into their test plan development. For the second TPWG, the TMP Director, Staff Advisors, and assigned Unit Test Safety Officer will review the updated draft test and safety plans. The focus will be on ensuring the team's test plan objectives, methodologies, and data analysis plans are well-constructed, and that the safety plan identifies and mitigates all risks associated with executing the test plan. Upon conclusion of the second TPWG, student teams will be able to incorporate feedback to have a finalized test plan and safety plan ready to submit to the technical and safety review boards. Graded test plans and safety plans will be submitted to the TPS-led Technical Review Board and the 412 TW SET-led Safety Review Board. The purpose of the Technical Review Board (TRB) is to have the student teams present their TMP test plans to the USAF TPS Technical Director (or delegate) and other appointed TRB members, to defend that the test plan is technically adequate, and answer the proposed TMP research question and customer's need. The TRB chair will provide a letter of technical adequacy to the Technical Execution Authority (TEA)/TPS Commandant. The Safety Review Board (SRB), led by a representative of the 412 TW Test Safety Office, is composed of a panel of Independent Operational Reviewers, Technical Experts, TPS Chief UTSO, assigned TMP UTSO, and potentially STARCOM DEL 12 Safety Officers. The purpose of the SRB is to provide a safety review and risk assessment of the finalized safety plan, which will be presented to the TEA/TPS Commandant. Upon completion of both the TRB and SRB, the students will document and correct any action items prior to proceeding with their request for test execution approval.

The A5 Program Manager will formally begin the 412 TW PID/SOC process by distributing the TCDs to the 412 TW POCs and non-AFTC organizations identified by each student team and requesting a JON. During the Core Team Meeting, the student teams will present their TCDs to the respective organizations, allowing the organizations an opportunity to either concur or non-concur with the support being requested. During the TMP planning process, students will have two test plan working groups (TPWGs) to assist them with drafting their test plan, safety plan, execution schedule, and any additional documentation as required by their TMP. The first TPWG will focus on defining the general test objectives (GTOs), specific test objectives (STOs), measures of performance (MOPs), and any outstanding technical planning discussions that will feed directly into their test plan development. For the second TPWG, the TMP Director, Staff Advisors, and assigned Unit Test Safety Officer will review the updated draft test and safety plans. The focus will be on ensuring the team's test plan objectives, methodologies, and data analysis plans are well-constructed, and that the safety plan identifies and mitigates all risks associated with executing the test plan. Upon conclusion of the second TPWG, student teams will be able to incorporate feedback to have a finalized test plan and safety plan ready to submit to the technical and safety review boards. Graded test plans and safety plans will be submitted to the TPS-led Technical Review Board and the 412 TW SET-led Safety Review Board. The purpose of the Technical Review Board (TRB) is to have the student teams present their TMP test plans to the USAF TPS Technical Director (or delegate) and other appointed TRB members, to defend that the test plan is technically adequate, and answer the proposed TMP research question and customer's need. The TRB chair will provide a letter of technical adequacy to the Technical Execution Authority (TEA)/TPS Commandant. The Safety Review Board (SRB), led by a representative of the 412 TW Test Safety Office, is composed of a panel of Independent Operational Reviewers, Technical Experts, TPS Chief UTSO, assigned TMP UTSO, and potentially STARCOM DEL 12 Safety Officers. The purpose of the SRB is to provide a safety review and risk assessment of the finalized safety plan, which will be presented to the TEA/TPS Commandant. Upon completion of both the TRB and SRB, the students will document and correct any action items prior to proceeding with their request for test execution approval.

TF 8201F.description: The purpose of the test missions is to collect data in accordance with the test plan and safety package to answer the customer’s questions.

TF 8301Y.description: The purpose of the first Program Assessment Review (PAR) is for each student TMP team to present their planned test objectives, test methodology, test schedule, and expected test results to their peers and TPS staff. The purpose of the Pre-Writers meeting is to brief students on the reporting expectations for their TMP, instruct them on how to write a TPS technical information memorandum (TIM), and to highlight the differences between the USAF TPS TMP TIM versus the 412 TW Technical Report. Upon conclusion of this meeting, student teams will be able to develop their draft TIMs. The second Program Assessment Review is intended to have the student teams brief their peers and TPS Staff on what occurred during their two-week TMP execution window. This will include the actual test execution schedule, preliminary status of data analysis, completion status of the test objectives and measures of performance, and any constraints or limitations that affected execution. The TMP Data Working Group is a post-execution, one-hour meeting with the student teams, TMP Director, Staff Advisor, and appropriate operations/technical experts to discuss data collected, analysis progress and way forward, and the anticipated scope of results and conclusions before completion of the final draft report for the TMP Report Coordination Meeting. The Report Coordination Meeting is a graded event for TMPs, equivalent to the 412 TW "murder board." The purpose of this meeting is to review the students' TIMs by conducting a line-by-line review, and discuss action items and comments required prior to submitting the final report for approval and signature by the TPS commandant and Technical Directors. The final signed and approved TMP TIM is an official USAF TPS document that will be submitted to the TMP customers. Depending on the distribution statement and classification of the TIM, the TIM may be submitted for Public Release Approval and stored in the Defense Technical Information Center and/or 412 TW Technical Library. The TMP final oral report is a graded 45-minute presentation by each of the student teams to brief their peers, AFTC, STARCOM, 412 TW, Delta 12, senior base leadership, and/or key stakeholders on their TMP results.

TF 9110M.description: Students will be evaluated on their ability to: 1) analyze a test scenario and clearly identify proper objectives that trace to data elements required to meet those objectives; 2) clearly identify the primary safety, security, technical, and programmatic risks; 3) demonstrate a clear understanding of the fundamental engineering principles related to test, translating program objectives and data requirements into proper test techniques and/or procedures; 4) translate test techniques and procedures into correct instrumentation and resource requirements; 5) demonstrate proper Test Judgement; and 6) effectively communicate and defend the test program and adapt it to unexpected circumstances. Students will think critically and apply skills and knowledge gained throughout the year to develop a plan for evaluating a system. Students must consider program requirements, test objectives, data requirements, test techniques, resources, and risks while properly scoping the test campaign with appropriate technical and safety buildup. Students must effectively communicate and defend their plan to a review board comprised of USAF Test Pilot School instructors.

TL 5101A.description: This section explores the ecosystem of defense innovation, focusing on the critical transition of technology from the laboratory to the warfighter ("crossing the Valley of Death"). Students will examine the specific roles and missions of key S&T organizations, such as the Air Force Research Lab (AFRL) and the Defense Advanced Research Projects Agency (DARPA). The course also investigates the impact of disruptive emerging technologies—such as Collaborative Combat Aircraft (CCAs) and Long Range Kill Chains (LRKC)—on future test ranges and methodologies.

TL 5102A.description: This block provides a comparative analysis of the strategic environments facing major geographic and functional Combatant Commands, with specific focus on EUCOM, INDOPACOM, SPACECOM, and CYBERCOM. Students will investigate how the unique geopolitical landscapes, physical environments, and adversary capabilities within each Area of Responsibility (AOR) drive specific requirements for system performance and test evaluation.

TL 5103A.description: This course section provides a foundational understanding of the Department of Defense acquisition framework. It covers the interaction between the Joint Capabilities Integration and Development System (JCIDS), the Defense Acquisition System, and the Planning, Programming, Budgeting, and Execution (PPBE) process. Special emphasis is placed on the "V-model" of systems engineering and the Tester's role in influencing requirements early in the lifecycle to ensure programs are structurally sound for eventual fielding.

TL 5104A.description: This module addresses the critical intersection of intelligence, security, and test execution. Students will receive necessary security indoctrination and gain insight into the capabilities of national intelligence assets. This will include briefs to cover technological capabilities and limitations of US and adversary forces.

TL 5105A.description: This section includes the formal indoctrination to the TPS Special Access Program (SAP) student baseline. Students will complete mandatory security training and receive detailed overviews of each program within the baseline to ensure full compliance and situational awareness for subsequent secure curriculum events.

TL 5110A.description: This block introduces students to formal Operational Test (OT) in the context of fielding new capabilities. An overview of the US laws governing OT and independent operational assessments will be presented. OT objectives and test considerations will be compared with those of Developmental Test (DT). Key OT terminology such as Effectiveness and Suitability will be presented, in addition to the different OT organizations and considerations for integrated testing will be discussed.

TL 6101A.description: This is a week-long block taught by instructors outside of TPS. Through a combination of lectures, discussions, and exercises, students will develop the skills and confidence needed to negotiate and communicate effectively with stakeholders, including program offices, contractors, and military personnel.

TL 6102Y.description: This is a week-long block taught by instructors outside of TPS. Through a combination of lectures, discussions, and exercises, students will develop the skills and confidence needed to negotiate and communicate effectively with stakeholders, including program offices, contractors, and military personnel.

TL 7101A.description: This is a week-long innovation block taught by instructors out of TPS. Through a combination of lectures, discussions, and exercises, students will develop the skills and knowledge needed to apply innovation principles and practices to real-world test and evaluation challenges.

TL 7102Y.description: This is a week-long innovation block taught by instructors out of TPS. Through a combination of lectures, discussions, and exercises, students will develop the skills and knowledge needed to apply innovation principles and practices to real-world test and evaluation challenges.

TL 7103Y.description: This is a week-long innovation block taught by instructors out of TPS. Through a combination of lectures, discussions, and exercises, students will develop the skills and knowledge needed to apply innovation principles and practices to real-world test and evaluation challenges.

TL 7201B.description: This block provides some basic computer programming skills required for the rest of the curriculum. These prerequisite courses will introduce you to the basics of coding in Python including basic arithmetic, data types, functions, logic, loops and working with popular external libraires. Learning these basics will enable you to complete hands on assignments in robotic perception, planning, and control. This block will also provide a foundation to understand the software driving modern air and space technologies.

TL 7210A.description: This block provides an overview of data-driven testing methods and outlines the journey to academia during the AI & Emerging Technology Trip.

TL 7220A.description: The students will participate in a 2-week trip to MIT or Standford which will provide a detailed overview of Data-Driven Test techniques and visits to industry. This immersive trip, a collaboration between the USAF Test Pilot School and a leading academic institution like MIT or Stanford, provides students with a comprehensive introduction to Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) for test and evaluation. The trip consists of academic instruction from university faculty on core topics, including AI fundamentals, data management, compute infrastructure, multimodal AI, and cybersecurity. A significant portion of the trip is dedicated to hands-on application through lab work, culminating in a capstone challenge. The experience is further enhanced by immersing students in the practical application of AI through tours of local technology companies and defense-focused labs, reinforcing the real-world relevance of the concepts learned. The overall goal is to equip future test professionals with the foundational knowledge and practical skills to design, evaluate, and implement AI/ML systems in complex operational environments.
